titleStmt.title: The Secretary of State to the Ambassador in the Soviet Union ( (Harriman))

div.head: The Secretary of State to the Ambassador in the Soviet Union ( Harriman )
div.p: 16. During the latter part of December Yugoslav Prime MinisterBozhidar Purich. stated to MacVeaghLincoln MacVeagh, Ambassador to the Yugoslav Government in Exile at Cairo; see his telegram 7, December 18, 1943, Foreign Relations, 1943, vol. ii, p. 1031. that the British AmbassadorR. C. Skrine Stevenson, British Ambassador to the Yugoslav Government in Exile at Cairo. had informed him that he could shortly expect joint pressure from the British and American governments to remove General MihailovitchDraza Mihailovich, Minister of War, Commander in Chief of the Yugoslav Armed Forces, and leader of the Chetnik resistance forces. from the Cabinet.The Assistant Secretary of State, Adolf A. Berle, Jr., wrote in a memorandum of January 1, 1944, that the Yugoslav Ambassador, Constantin Fotich, had expressed the hope that “no final decision would be taken until we likewise had a report from our representative with General Mikhailovitch’s forces.” The Assistant Secretary replied that “of course we wanted all the information we could get on all angles of the situation.” (740.60H114/26) Since the Department had not been consulted by the British government regarding the Yugoslav situation nor had it instructed Ambassador MacVeagh to approach the Yugoslav government in the sense indicated the Ambassador at LondonJohn G. Winant, American Ambassador in the United Kingdom. was instructed to ascertain from the British Foreign Office the exact tenor of the British Ambassador’s remark to the Yugoslav Prime Minister.See telegram 8141, December 24, 1943, to London, Foreign Relations, 1943, vol. ii, p. 1036.

MacVeagh reports that the British Ambassador at Cairo evidently as a result of this approach has explained in some embarrassment that his remark to the Yugoslav Prime Minister was simply a general one to the effect that American and British policy must coincide. MacVeagh, however, believes that the Prime Minister understood him to imply that a joint policy had been decided upon. MacVeagh believes
that the Department’s action may be helpful in clearing up an apparent tendency on the part of the British Ambassador to the Yugoslav and Greek governments to treat keeping him informed of their plans as tantamount to securing American association therewith.
